timeline.label: Enterprise
timeline.text: F500 Automotive, Global Tech, Global Logistics, Enterprise Healthcare — BA, product owner, delivery lead

timeline.label: Education
timeline.text: After a decade of enterprise delivery (F500 Automotive, Dell), I returned to academia to master the human factors of AI. Most companies focus entirely on the LLM technology and ignore the psychology of user adoption—which is why enterprise AI fails. Texas Tech B.S. Human-Centered AI (expected 2026) · Cornell AI Certification

timeline.label: Building
timeline.text: TradePilot (supply chain) + TalentPilot (recruiting) — same architecture, different domains

work.title: TradePilot
work.summary: Decision intelligence for supply chain. AI agents detect risk, quantify exposure, and recommend — with full transparency.
work.desc: Supply chains don't fail because of bad data. They fail because the right signal reaches the wrong person too late. TradePilot puts AI agents between the signal and the decision, with Glass Box transparency so you can see exactly why the system recommends what it recommends.
work.problem: Supply chain decisions are reactive, fragmented, and opaque. When a tariff changes or a supplier misses a deadline, the response depends on who happens to notice first. Cross-department impact is invisible until it hits the P&L.
work.approach: Built a decision intelligence layer with 5 specialized AI agents — each monitoring different signal sources (trade policy, logistics, payments, inventory, sourcing). Agents detect, quantify in dollars, and recommend. Humans review through a Glass Box evidence chain and approve or override. The 10-80-10 framework: humans set thresholds (10%), AI handles monitoring and analysis (80%), humans make final decisions (10%).

detail.heroStatement: Decision intelligence for supply chain. When tariffs change or suppliers miss deadlines, TradePilot's AI agents detect, quantify, and recommend — with full transparency into why. Built on a 'Neuro-Symbolic' architecture: we use deterministic code to bound and cage the probabilistic LLM. Human-configured guardrails and Glass Box compliance checks ensure the AI never executes a final action.

architectureSnapshot.framework: 10-80-10: Humans set thresholds (10%) → AI agents monitor & analyze (80%) → Humans review & approve (10%)

patternTransfer.text: Same architecture that powers TalentPilot for recruiting. Different domain, same decision intelligence patterns.

caseStudy.title: When Tariffs Hit: A Supply Chain Simulation
caseStudy.subtitle: What happens when a 15% tariff increase hits your Chinese suppliers overnight — and your system detects it before your team arrives at work.

sections.heading: The Scenario
sections.body: At 2:47 AM, the Federal Register publishes a final rule: 15% tariff increase on Chinese-manufactured steel components, effective in 30 days. By 6:15 AM, before Sarah Chen (VP Supply Chain Operations) has opened her laptop, TradePilot's Trade Policy Analyst agent has already parsed the filing, cross-referenced it against 847 active purchase orders, and identified three with direct exposure.

sections.heading: How TradePilot Responds
sections.body:
Hour 1 — Detection & Quantification: Three purchase orders flagged. PO-1045 ($45,000 steel housings from Shenzhen Metalworks), PO-1046 ($38,500 precision bearings from Guangzhou Industrial), PO-1047 ($29,000 connector assemblies from Suzhou Components). Combined exposure: $112,500. The system doesn't stop at identification. Each PO gets a confidence score — 95% for PO-1045 (direct tariff code match), 87% for PO-1046 (component contains Chinese-origin steel), 72% for PO-1047 (assembly uses tariff-affected subcomponents, requires supply chain verification).

Hour 3 — Cross-Department Ripple: The Logistics Agent flags that PO-1045 shares a container booking with two unaffected orders — rebooking the container to reroute sourcing would impact $23,000 in other shipments. The AP Controller calculates payment timing: $67,000 in payments are scheduled within the 30-day window, creating a cash flow decision point. Inventory Guard identifies that current safety stock covers 18 days of production at current run rate — sourcing alternatives need to be locked in within 12 days to avoid line stoppage.

Hour 6 — Decision Package: When Sarah opens TradePilot, she doesn't see an alert. She sees a complete decision package: the exposure ($112,500), the cross-department impact ($79,000 in ripple effects across logistics, AP, and inventory), and a 4-step recommended action plan with assigned owners and deadlines.

sections.heading: The Counterfactual
sections.body: Without TradePilot, here's the realistic timeline: Day 1-3: Someone in procurement reads a trade publication article about the tariff change. Day 4-7: Manual review of supplier contracts to identify affected POs. Day 8-12: Cross-department emails to assess logistics, payment, and inventory impact. Day 14-18: First meeting with all stakeholders in the same room. Day 20+: Decision made, action initiated — with 10 days until the tariff takes effect. The 6-hour detection and analysis that TradePilot completed would take 2-3 weeks through normal enterprise channels.

sections.heading: The Economics of Speed
sections.body: The difference isn't just time. It's optionality. At hour 6, Sarah has 29 days and 18 hours to act. She can renegotiate with existing suppliers (strongest position — they haven't been approached by competitors yet). She can activate pre-qualified alternative suppliers. She can adjust inventory orders to build buffer stock at current prices. She can restructure the container booking to optimize logistics costs. At day 20 (the manual timeline), most of these options are gone. Alternative suppliers have been approached by competitors. Current-price inventory windows have closed. The container booking is locked. The $112,500 in direct exposure becomes $112,500 plus an unknowable amount in lost optionality.

sections.heading: What This Demonstrates
sections.body:
This isn't a case study — it's a simulation built on real-world parameters. The tariff scenario is modeled on actual 2024-2025 trade policy changes. The PO amounts reflect realistic mid-market procurement volumes. The agent response chain (detection → quantification → cross-department analysis → decision package) runs in the live prototype.

The question this prototype answers: can an AI-augmented decision layer compress the enterprise response cycle from weeks to hours? The answer, at least at the prototype level: yes. The next question — can it do it with real supplier data, real ERP integration, and real procurement workflows — is what a 4-week pilot engagement would validate.

work.title: TalentPilot
work.summary: Same architecture, different domain. Decision intelligence for recruiting with native bias monitoring.
work.desc: Same architecture, different domain. Recruiting has the same broken pattern: decisions made on incomplete data, bias invisible in the process, institutional knowledge siloed. TalentPilot tests whether the framework holds when you swap the industry.
work.problem: Recruiting decisions are made on incomplete data, bias is invisible in the process, and institutional knowledge about what makes a good hire is siloed in individual recruiters' heads.
work.approach: Applied the same architecture from TradePilot to recruiting. Decision enrichment replaces candidate scoring (because scoring implies the AI makes the call). Bias monitoring is built into the decision flow, not bolted on as a report. 4 AI agents, same Glass Box transparency.

detail.heroStatement: Decision intelligence for recruiting. TalentPilot sits between AI matching and human judgment — enriching every recommendation with bias checks, compliance gates, and evidence chains. Built on a 'Neuro-Symbolic' architecture: we use deterministic code to bound and cage the probabilistic LLM. Human-configured guardrails and Glass Box compliance checks ensure the AI never executes a final action.

architectureSnapshot.framework: 10-80-10: Recruiters configure agents & set guardrails (10%) → AI agents scan, enrich, and monitor (80%) → Recruiters review through Glass Box & decide (10%)

patternTransfer.text: Same architecture that powers TradePilot for supply chain. Different domain, same decision intelligence patterns.

LL 144 notice and IL audit prep surfaced proactively
across 4 clients — active decisions managed with full evidence chains

differentiator.text: Unlike most AI recruiting platforms, bias monitoring isn't an add-on — it's a core view. The Bias Dashboard runs counter-factual analysis, proxy variable detection, and 4/5ths rule checks on every decision, in real time.
differentiator.label: What makes this different

scenarios.heading: What TalentPilot Catches
scenarios.intro: These scenarios walk through what the prototype actually demonstrates — specific situations where the system surfaces problems before they become expensive.

items.title: Bias Detection Before It Becomes a Problem
items.body: A senior ML engineer opening shows an 87% match confidence for Marcus Rivera. But the Bias Dashboard flags that the engineering pipeline has a gender disparity drift. TalentPilot surfaces this at point of decision — before the hire, not in a quarterly audit. The recruiter sees the flag, the counter-factual analysis, and the evidence chain. They can adjust or document their rationale.

items.title: Compliance Surfaced Proactively
items.body: An LL 144 notice and IL audit prep deadline are approaching. Instead of the compliance team discovering this reactively, the Compliance Guardian agent surfaces these as active compliance actions with deadlines and required steps. The recruiter and account manager see the impact on their active pipeline before it becomes a fire drill.

architectureComparison.title: One accountability framework. Two industries.
architectureComparison.body: Built for the moment a human decides whether to trust it. The same architecture powers both products because it was designed around the decision, not the domain.
architectureComparison.closing: The domain changes. The architecture doesn't.

rows.left: 5 agents: Trade Policy, Logistics, AP Controller, Inventory, Sourcing
rows.right: 4 agents: Signal Scout, Enrichment, Pipeline Monitor, Compliance

rows.left: Federal Register sources, supplier data, 95% confidence scoring

rows.left: Approve, reject, or override with audit trail
